summary:
The Great Turkish War (1683–1699 AD) was the decisive military conflict between the Holy League (the Habsburg Empire, Venice, Poland, and later Russia) and the [[event:2400|Ottoman Empire]] that ended over a century of Ottoman offensive pressure on Central Europe and initiated the long Ottoman retreat from Europe. The war began with the Ottoman Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa Pasha's massive invasion and siege of Vienna in July 1683 — the second Ottoman attempt to take the Habsburg capital (the first was the [[event:2323|Siege of Vienna]] in 1529). The siege was broken on September 12, 1683 by a relief army of Habsburg, German, and Polish forces under King John III Sobieski of Poland, whose cavalry charge at the Battle of Vienna was one of the largest cavalry charges in European history.

Following the relief of Vienna, the Holy League took the offensive for the first time — a sustained counteroffensive that lasted 16 years. The Habsburg general Prince Eugene of Savoy's victory at the Battle of Zenta (1697) was the campaign's decisive engagement, and the subsequent [[event:2468|Treaty of Karlowitz]] (1699) transferred Hungary, Transylvania, and other territories to the Habsburg Empire, Poland recovered Podolia, and Venice gained the Peloponnese and Dalmatia. The Great Turkish War thus ended the era of Ottoman military superiority in Central Europe and began the long 'Eastern Question' — the geopolitical problem of managing Ottoman decline — that would preoccupy European diplomacy for the next two centuries. The Battle of Vienna is sometimes cited as a 'turning point' in Christian-Muslim civilizational conflict, a framing contested by historians who prefer to see it as one episode in complex state relations.
